headline: AI platforms Hazel and Avantos pressure bank wealth units to modernize as independent channel accelerates
summary: Altruist's launch of Hazel, an AI-based tax planning capability built via its 2025 acquisition of startup Thyme, triggered an estimated $130 billion market value wipeout across Raymond James, Charles Schwab, and LPL Financial within two days, while bank wealth divisions were largely spared due to structural insulation. Hazel combines meeting intelligence, live custodial data, and tax planning into a single advisor environment, enabling productivity expansion and proactive next-best-action engagement for advisors managing large, heterogeneous books. Avantos, a recently launched AI-native platform for institutional wealth management, layers a knowledge graph across core systems to enable advisors, servicing teams, and AI agents to operate with shared context, with Mercer Advisors reporting active deployment across tens of thousands of clients. Bank wealth units face three key investment areas to capture this opportunity: data completeness, supervisory workflow with compliance architecture, and change management for captive advisor adoption. Datos Insights positions Hazel as a competitive reference point for bank wealth leaders and Avantos as the more actionable institutional-scale opportunity.

key_stat.label: Estimated market value wiped out within two days of Hazel's launch across Raymond James, Charles Schwab, and LPL Financial

verified_claims: Altruist launched Hazel's AI-based tax planning capability in February
Raymond James, Charles Schwab, and LPL Financial suffered major declines following Hazel's launch
The selloff wiped out an estimated $130 billion in market value within two days
Bank wealth divisions were largely spared from the selloff
Hazel was built by Altruist through its 2025 acquisition of AI startup Thyme
Hazel combines meeting intelligence, live custodial data, and tax planning into a single advisor environment
Hazel enables proactive next-best-action engagement for advisors
Avantos is a recently launched AI-native platform for institutional wealth management
Avantos layers a knowledge graph across core systems
Avantos enables advisors, servicing teams, and AI agents to operate with shared context
Mercer Advisors reports active deployment across tens of thousands of clients
Three key investment areas identified: data completeness, supervisory workflow with compliance architecture, and change management for captive advisor adoption
Datos Insights positions Hazel as a competitive reference point for bank wealth leaders
Datos Insights positions Avantos as the more actionable institutional-scale opportunity
The article's author is William Trout, Director, Securities and Investments, Datos Insights
fabricated_claims: The headline and summary describe the startup acquired as 'Thyme' — the source confirms this name, so no fabrication there; however, the summary states Hazel enables 'productivity expansion' which is accurate but the framing of 'managing large, heterogeneous books' is a slight editorial addition not directly contradicted but not explicitly sourced to Datos Insights as a formal characterization
_governance.notes: The entry is largely faithful to the source. The $130B figure, the companies named, the Thyme acquisition, Avantos details, Mercer Advisors deployment, the three investment areas, and the Datos Insights framing all verify accurately. One minor concern: the summary's phrasing 'startup Thyme' is confirmed in the source. The headline references 'independent channel accelerates' which is editorially consistent with the source's themes. No materially fabricated or contradicted claims were identified. The entry is an accurate and tight paraphrase of the source article.
_governance.note: Full article text provided by user; Jina reader missed opening section
